stem: In a school performance study comparing School A vs. School B: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher test score improvement (65.0% vs 58.3%). However, when stratified by socioeconomic background: In affluent area students (n_X=40, n_Y=100): Treatment X achieves 87% vs Treatment Y's 77%. In lower-income area students (n_X=80, n_Y=40): Treatment X achieves 44% vs Treatment Y's 35%. The true population proportion of affluent area students is uncertain, estimated between 36% and 52%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to lower-income area students (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.094, 0.096], point estimate: 0.094. Treatment X is genuinely better by 9.4 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=58.3%, Y=65.0% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In affluent area students: X=87% > Y=77%. In lower-income area students: X=44% > Y=35%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because School A is applied more to lower-income area students (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
